project: VesselTree.jl — Physiologically Accurate Vascular Tree Generator
goal: Generate vascular trees from large arteries (3-5mm) to capillaries (8um) using CCO + Kassab morphometry + Barabasi surface optimization. ALL computational kernels use AcceleratedKernels.jl for native CPU/GPU performance. General-purpose (coronary, cerebral, pulmonary) with coronary as initial target.

architecture.compute: AcceleratedKernels.jl for ALL parallel kernels. No plain for-loops on segment arrays.
architecture.data_layout: Structure-of-Arrays (SoA) for geometry. Topology in plain Vectors on CPU.
architecture.science: CCO growth + Kassab morphometry (gamma=7/3, asymmetry Beta(2.5,0.8)) + Barabasi surface optimization (chi/rho thresholds at 0.83)

stories.title: DISCOVERY: AcceleratedKernels.jl API for tree algorithms
stories.completedNote: All 10 research questions answered with verified code snippets. Key findings: no argmin/argmax (use mapreduce), merge_sort_by_key GPU-only, SoA+views confirmed working, CPU crossover at ~100K elements, closures must be in function scope for GPU.
stories.description:
Deep-dive into AcceleratedKernels.jl to understand how to use it for the specific computational patterns in vascular tree generation.

Research:
1. AK.foreachindex — element-wise parallel operations on arrays
2. AK.reduce, AK.mapreduce — parallel reductions (min, sum, argmin)
3. AK.accumulate — prefix sums (useful for level-wise processing?)
4. AK.sort, AK.sortperm — parallel sorting (spatial bucketing?)
5. Backend support — what works on CPU, CUDA, Metal, oneAPI?
6. How to handle growing arrays (tree adds segments over time) — pre-allocate with capacity?
7. SoA vs AoS — what layout works best with AK kernels?
8. Can AK kernels close over external arrays (capture in closure)?
9. Error handling inside AK kernels (what happens on NaN, out-of-bounds?)
10. Performance: overhead of AK.foreachindex for small arrays (<100 elements)?

Output: `ralph_loop/research/ak-api.md`

Do NOT write any VesselTree.jl source code.
acceptanceCriteria: Research document covers all 10 questions above
Working code snippets tested locally for each AK pattern
Recommendation for SoA layout that works with AK
Recommendation for growing-array strategy (pre-allocate + n counter)
No source code written in src/ or test/

stories.title: DISCOVERY: OpenCCO algorithm deep-dive
stories.completedNote: Full algorithm documented from IPOL paper + source code. Step-by-step growth loop, Kamiya optimization with equations, intersection testing, cost function, radius propagation, data structure, and scaling analysis. Key differences from VesselTree identified (SoA vs AoS, surface vs volume cost, gamma=7/3 vs 3.0, trifurcation support).
stories.description:
Study the OpenCCO C++ codebase (https://github.com/OpenCCO-team/OpenCCO) to understand the CCO algorithm in detail.

Key files to read:
- CoronaryArteryTree.cpp/h — Tree data structure and main growth logic
- GeomHelpers.cpp/h — Kamiya optimization (bifurcation point finding)
- ExpandTreeHelpers.cpp/h — Growth loop, candidate sampling, connection selection
- DomainController.cpp/h — Perfusion domain boundary handling

Questions to answer:
1. How does the main growth loop work step-by-step?
2. How does Kamiya optimization find the optimal bifurcation point?
3. How are candidate terminal points sampled and filtered?
4. How are segment intersections detected?
5. How does the cost function work (volume minimization)?
6. How does radius propagation work after adding a bifurcation?
7. How is the tree stored in memory? (Arrays? Linked list?)
8. How is the domain boundary handled (rejection sampling? SDF?)
9. What are the key performance bottlenecks and how are they addressed?
10. How many terminals can OpenCCO handle? What's the scaling?

Also read the IPOL paper (doi:10.5201/ipol.2023.477) for the mathematical details.

Output: `ralph_loop/research/opencco-algorithm.md`
acceptanceCriteria: Step-by-step algorithm documented with pseudocode
Kamiya optimization explained with math
Data structure design documented
Performance characteristics noted
Key differences from our approach identified (we use surface cost, not volume)

stories.title: DISCOVERY: Kassab 1993 morphometry data extraction
stories.completedNote: Complete morphometric tables extracted: diameter/length by order 0-11 with SD, diameter boundary thresholds, approximate connectivity matrix, asymmetry distribution Beta(2.5,0.8), segment counts (~6M per artery), L/D ratios, branching angles, gamma=7/3 derivation summary. Validation criteria defined.
stories.description:
Extract and document ALL quantitative data from Kassab et al. 1993 (Am J Physiol Heart 265:H350-H365) and related Kassab/Huo papers.

Data to extract:
1. Table 1: Diameter by Strahler order (mean, SD, N for each order 0-11)
2. Table 2: Segment length by order (mean, SD, N)
3. Table 3: Connectivity matrix (probability of order m parent having order n daughter)
4. Asymmetry ratio distribution (Ds/Dl statistics)
5. Branching angle measurements (mean, SD by order)
6. Total segment counts by order
7. Length/Diameter ratios by order
8. Element vs segment distinction (Kassab's terminology)
9. Diameter-defined Strahler ordering rules (threshold boundaries)
10. From Huo-Kassab 2007: derivation summary of gamma = 7/3

Also check: Are the Kassab tables available digitized anywhere? If not, transcribe from the paper.

Output: `ralph_loop/research/kassab-morphometry.md` with complete tables.

This data becomes the GROUND TRUTH for validation.
acceptanceCriteria: Complete diameter table (orders 0-11) with mean, SD, N
Complete length table with mean, SD, N
Connectivity matrix transcribed
Asymmetry ratio distribution parameters documented
Diameter boundary thresholds for order classification
Total segment counts (approximately 2 million per major artery)
gamma = 7/3 derivation summarized from Huo-Kassab 2007

stories.title: DISCOVERY: Barabasi 2026 surface optimization for blood vessels
stories.completedNote: Surface optimization fully documented: chi parameter (w/r) with trifurcation transition at 0.83, rho parameter (r_small/r_large) with sprouting/branching transition at 0.83, junction surface computation via pants decomposition, P(lambda) distribution, blood vessel data (8.3% trifurcations, 25.6% sprouting). Implementation strategy: use threshold rules instead of PDE solver. min-surf-netw is Mathematica (not Python).
stories.description:
Study Barabasi et al. 2026 (Nature 649:315-322) and the min-surf-netw code (https://github.com/Barabasi-Lab/min-surf-netw).

Key concepts to understand and document:
1. Surface minimization vs length/volume minimization — why surface?
2. The chi parameter (chi = w/r): physical meaning, how to compute
3. Trifurcation stability: when chi > 0.83, k=4 nodes are stable
4. The rho parameter (rho = D_small/D_large): sprouting vs branching
5. Sprouting regime (rho < rho_th): perpendicular branch emergence
6. Branching regime (rho >= rho_th): both daughters deflect
7. rho_th for blood vessels specifically (0.83 from paper)
8. The P(lambda) distribution: what it means, expected shape for blood vessels
9. Junction surface computation: pants decomposition, minimal surfaces
10. How does this modify Murray's law? Connection to gamma != 3.0
11. Data from physical.network — what blood vessel data is available?

Study the Python code in min-surf-netw for implementation details:
- How is the surface cost computed numerically?
- How are junction geometries optimized?
- What simplifications can we use for tube-like vessels?

Output: `ralph_loop/research/barabasi-surface.md`
acceptanceCriteria: Chi parameter explained with formula and physical meaning
Rho parameter and sprouting/branching transition documented
Trifurcation stability criterion documented
Junction surface cost computation approach identified
Blood vessel-specific parameter values extracted
P(lambda) distribution shape for blood vessels documented
Implementation strategy recommended for our use case

stories.title: Project setup: Package skeleton with AK.jl
stories.completedNote: Package skeleton created with Project.toml (AcceleratedKernels, Distributions, StaticArrays, LinearAlgebra, Random), src/VesselTree.jl module with 9 scientific constants (gamma=7/3, cutoff=8um, Barabasi thresholds), test/runtests.jl with 21 passing tests (module loading, constants, AK operations), CLAUDE.md with project context and conventions. BitVector gotcha: AK requires Vector{Bool}.

Create the VesselTree.jl package skeleton with AcceleratedKernels.jl as a core dependency.

Create:

stories.title: Core types: SoA segment storage + tree topology
stories.description:
Implement the core data structures using SoA layout for AK compatibility.

Create src/types.jl with:

1. SegmentData{V} — SoA storage for segment geometry
   - proximal_x/y/z, distal_x/y/z (V <: AbstractVector — works with CuArray etc)
   - radius, seg_length (V)
   - flow, pressure_proximal, pressure_distal, resistance (V)
   - n::Int (active count), capacity::Int

2. TreeTopology — CPU-only topology data
   - parent_id::Vector{Int32} (-1 for root)
   - child1_id::Vector{Int32} (-1 for leaf)
   - child2_id::Vector{Int32} (-1 for leaf)
   - child3_id::Vector{Int32} (-1 for non-trifurcation)
   - strahler_order::Vector{Int32}
   - generation::Vector{Int32}
   - is_terminal::Vector{Bool}
   - junction_type::Vector{Symbol} (:none, :bifurcation, :trifurcation)

3. VascularTree — composite
   - name::String
   - segments::SegmentData{V}
   - topology::TreeTopology
   - root_segment_id::Int32
   - n_terminals::Int, n_bifurcations::Int, n_trifurcations::Int

4. Constructor functions:
   - SegmentData(capacity::Int) — pre-allocate with given capacity
   - add_segment!(tree, proximal, distal, radius, parent_id) -> segment_id
   - get_children(topology, segment_id) -> Vector{Int32}

Create test/test_types.jl with thorough tests:
   - Construction with various capacities
   - Adding segments and verifying SoA arrays update correctly
   - Topology parent/child relationships
   - Edge cases: empty tree, single segment, capacity growth
acceptanceCriteria: SegmentData uses SoA layout with parameterized vector type V
TreeTopology stores parent/child/order data in plain Vectors
VascularTree composes SegmentData + TreeTopology
add_segment! correctly updates both SoA geometry and topology
Pre-allocation with capacity works (n < capacity)
At least 20 tests in test_types.jl, all passing

stories.title: Morphometric parameters + domain types
stories.description:
Implement parameter structs and perfusion domain abstraction.

Create src/parameters.jl:

- Fields: gamma (7/3), diameter_by_order, length_by_order, diameter_bounds, connectivity_matrix, asymmetry_distribution (Beta(2.5,0.8)), trifurcation_threshold (0.83), sprouting_threshold (0.83), blood_viscosity, root_pressure, terminal_pressure, min_segment_length, angle_mean, angle_std
- build_connectivity_matrix() — from Kassab 1993 data

- EllipsoidDomain — parameterized ellipsoid (good for LV myocardium)

Create tests for both.
acceptanceCriteria: MorphometricParams struct holds all required fields
kassab_coronary_params() returns correct values (gamma=7/3, all Kassab tables)
Connectivity matrix is valid (rows sum to ~1.0)
At least 3 domain types implemented with in_domain + sample_point
signed_distance works for boundary proximity testing
At least 20 tests passing for parameters + domains

stories.title: AK-accelerated distance kernels
stories.description:
Implement point-to-segment distance computation using AK.foreachindex.

This is THE hot path — called for every candidate terminal against every existing segment.

Create src/distance.jl:

1. point_segment_distance_scalar(cx, cy, cz, px, py, pz, dx, dy, dz) -> Float64
   Pure scalar function computing minimum distance from point (c) to line segment (p->d).
   This is the kernel that runs inside AK.foreachindex.

2. compute_all_distances!(distances, segments::SegmentData, cx, cy, cz)
   AK.foreachindex over all segments, computing distance from candidate to each segment.
   distances[i] = point_segment_distance_scalar(cx, cy, cz, segments.proximal_x[i], ...)

3. find_nearest_segments(distances, k) -> indices
   Return indices of k nearest segments (for candidate connection search).
   Use AK.sortperm or partial sort.

Create test/test_distance.jl:
- Test scalar distance function against known geometry (point on line, point off line, point at endpoint)
- Test AK kernel against brute-force loop on 1000+ segments
- Test with collinear points, zero-length segments, very far points
- Verify find_nearest returns correct k segments
acceptanceCriteria: Scalar distance function is correct for all geometric cases
AK kernel produces same results as scalar loop to within Float64 precision
find_nearest_segments returns correct indices
Works on arrays of 1000+ segments
No plain for-loops in distance.jl (AK only)
At least 15 tests passing

stories.title: AK-accelerated intersection testing
stories.description:
Implement segment-segment intersection testing using AK.foreachindex.

When adding a new segment, we must verify it doesn't cross any existing segment.

Create src/intersection.jl:

1. segments_intersect_scalar(a1x,a1y,a1z, a2x,a2y,a2z, b1x,b1y,b1z, b2x,b2y,b2z, min_dist) -> Bool
   Returns true if the minimum distance between two 3D line segments is less than min_dist.
   Uses the standard closest-point-on-two-lines algorithm.

2. check_intersections!(results, segments::SegmentData, new_px,new_py,new_pz, new_dx,new_dy,new_dz, min_dist)
   AK.foreachindex kernel: results[i] = true if segment i intersects the new segment.

3. has_any_intersection(results) -> Bool
   Quick check using AK.reduce(|, results) or AK.any.

4. check_domain_crossing(domain, p1, p2) -> Bool
   Check if a segment from p1 to p2 leaves the domain (sample points along segment).

Create test/test_intersection.jl:
- Known intersecting segments (crossing X pattern)
- Known non-intersecting segments (parallel, skew)
- Edge cases: shared endpoint, collinear, zero-length
- AK kernel vs scalar loop verification on 1000+ segments
- Domain crossing detection
acceptanceCriteria: Scalar intersection test handles all geometric edge cases
AK kernel correctly flags all intersecting segments
has_any_intersection uses AK reduction, not loop
Domain crossing detection works with EllipsoidDomain
No plain for-loops in intersection.jl
At least 15 tests passing

stories.title: Kamiya bifurcation point optimization
stories.description:
Implement the Kamiya optimization: given a parent segment A->B and a new terminal point T, find the optimal bifurcation point P along AB that minimizes surface cost.

Create src/kamiya.jl:

1. surface_cost_at_t(t, segment_data, seg_idx, terminal_x/y/z, params) -> Float64
   Compute surface cost for bifurcation at parametric position t in [0,1] along segment.
   Surface cost = 2*pi*r1*L1 + 2*pi*r2*L2 + 2*pi*r3*L3 + junction_surface_estimate
   where r1,r2,r3 are estimated via Murray's law.

2. optimize_bifurcation_point(segment_data, seg_idx, terminal_x/y/z, params) -> (t_opt, cost)
   Golden section search along segment to minimize surface cost.
   Returns optimal parametric position and cost.

3. evaluate_all_connections!(costs, bifurc_t, segments, n_active, tx, ty, tz, params)
   AK.foreachindex kernel: for each segment, run Kamiya optimization and store cost + bifurcation position.
   This is the main connection-finding kernel.

4. select_best_connection(costs, bifurc_t, n_active) -> (segment_idx, t_opt, cost)
   Find the connection with minimum cost.

Note: The golden section search per segment runs INSIDE the AK kernel (each thread optimizes one segment independently).

Create test/test_kamiya.jl with geometric test cases.
acceptanceCriteria: Golden section search converges to correct bifurcation point on known geometry
Surface cost function correctly combines cylinder surfaces + junction estimate
AK kernel evaluates all connections in parallel
Optimal connection matches brute-force search
No plain for-loops in kamiya.jl
At least 15 tests passing

stories.title: Core CCO growth loop + Murray's law propagation
stories.description:
Implement the main CCO growth algorithm that adds terminal segments to the tree.

Create src/growth.jl:

1. grow_tree!(tree, domain, n_terminals, params; rng=Random.default_rng()) -> VascularTree
   Main loop (SEQUENTIAL — inherently serial, one terminal at a time):
   For each new terminal:
   a. Sample candidate point in domain (rejection sampling)
   b. Find optimal connection using AK-parallel Kamiya evaluation
   c. Check intersection constraints using AK-parallel intersection test
   d. If valid: add bifurcation (split parent segment, create two daughters)
   e. Update radii via Murray's law
   f. Log progress every 100 terminals

2. add_bifurcation!(tree, parent_seg_idx, bifurc_t, terminal_point) -> (new_seg1_id, new_seg2_id)
   Split parent segment at bifurcation point. Create two new daughter segments.
   Update topology (parent/child relationships).

3. sample_terminal_candidate(domain, tree, params, rng) -> (x, y, z) or nothing
   Rejection sampling: random point in domain, reject if too close to existing segments.
   Uses AK distance kernel for proximity check.

Create src/murray.jl:

1. update_radii!(tree, params)
   Propagate radii from terminals to root using Murray's law: r_parent^gamma = sum(r_child^gamma)
   Process in reverse topological order (terminals first, root last).
   The per-level updates use AK.foreachindex; the level traversal is sequential.

2. compute_terminal_radius(flow, terminal_pressure, length, viscosity) -> Float64
   Determine terminal segment radius from hemodynamic constraints.

Create test/test_growth.jl:
- Grow a small tree (100 terminals) in a SphereDomain
- Verify tree connectivity (every segment has valid parent except root)
- Verify Murray's law holds at every bifurcation
- Verify no segment intersections
- Verify all terminals are in domain
- Verify segment count == 2*n_terminals - 1 (binary tree property)
acceptanceCriteria: grow_tree! produces a valid tree with n_terminals leaves
Murray's law r^gamma = r1^gamma + r2^gamma holds at every bifurcation (rtol=1e-10)
No segment intersections in the generated tree
All segment endpoints are within the domain
Segment count = 2*n_terminals - 1
AK kernels used for distance, intersection, and connection finding
Can grow 500+ terminal tree without error
At least 20 tests passing

stories.title: Spatial indexing for fast segment queries
stories.description:
The naive O(n) scan of all segments for distance/intersection becomes the bottleneck as the tree grows. Implement spatial indexing to reduce the candidate set.

Create src/spatial.jl:

1. SpatialGrid — 3D uniform grid that buckets segment midpoints
   - build_grid(segments, domain, cell_size) -> SpatialGrid
   - query_nearby(grid, point, search_radius) -> Vector{Int32} (segment indices)
   - insert!(grid, segment_idx, midpoint)

2. The growth loop should:
   a. First query the spatial grid for nearby segments (cheap)
   b. Then run AK distance/intersection kernels only on the nearby subset
   c. This reduces from O(n) to O(k) where k << n

3. Update grow_tree! to use spatial indexing when n_segments > threshold (e.g., 500).

Create test/test_spatial.jl:
- Build grid, insert segments, verify query returns correct neighbors
- Verify no false negatives (nearest segment is always in query result)
- Performance: query should be faster than full scan for n > 1000
acceptanceCriteria: SpatialGrid correctly indexes segment midpoints
query_nearby returns all segments within search_radius
No false negatives (nearest segment always included)
grow_tree! uses spatial grid when n > threshold
At least 10 tests passing

stories.title: Hemodynamics: Poiseuille resistance + pressure/flow
stories.description:
Implement hemodynamic computations using AK kernels.

Create src/hemodynamics.jl:

1. compute_resistances!(segments, viscosity)
   AK kernel: R[i] = 8 * mu * L[i] / (pi * r[i]^4)

2. compute_flows!(tree, params)
   Assign flows from terminals to root.
   Terminal flow = total_flow / n_terminals (equal distribution initially).
   At bifurcations: Q_parent = Q_child1 + Q_child2.
   Sequential tree walk (root to terminal), but per-level flow assignment uses AK.

3. compute_pressures!(tree, params)
   From root pressure, compute pressure drop along each segment:
   P_distal = P_proximal - Q * R
   Sequential tree walk from root to terminals.

4. validate_hemodynamics(tree, params) -> Bool
   Check: all pressures in [0, root_pressure], all flows > 0, terminal pressures near target.

Create test/test_hemodynamics.jl:
- Single straight tube: verify Poiseuille formula
- Simple bifurcation: verify flow conservation
- Small tree: verify pressures decrease from root to terminals
- Validate hemodynamics passes on a CCO-generated tree
acceptanceCriteria: Poiseuille resistance computed via AK kernel
Flow conservation holds at every bifurcation
Pressure monotonically decreases root-to-terminal
Terminal pressures are near params.terminal_pressure
validate_hemodynamics returns true for valid trees
At least 15 tests passing

stories.title: Flow-weighted radius assignment
stories.description:
Enhance the hemodynamic model with flow-weighted radius assignment instead of equal terminal flow.

In a real tree, terminal segments serve different tissue volumes. Segments deeper in the myocardium or serving larger territories should get more flow.

Update src/hemodynamics.jl:

1. assign_terminal_flows!(tree, domain, params)
   Weight terminal flow by the estimated tissue volume each terminal serves.
   Use Voronoi-like territory: each terminal's flow proportional to its territory volume.
   AK kernel: for each terminal, estimate territory volume (approximate by nearest-neighbor distance).

2. recompute_radii_from_flow!(tree, params)
   Given new flow distribution, recompute radii using Murray's law.
   This creates more realistic radius variation.

Create additional tests for flow-weighted assignment.
acceptanceCriteria: Terminal flows are weighted by territory, not uniform
Radii update correctly from flow-weighted assignment
Murray's law still holds at every bifurcation
Total flow is conserved
At least 10 tests passing

stories.title: Kassab: Strahler ordering + asymmetry sampling
stories.description:
Implement Kassab's diameter-defined Strahler ordering and asymmetry ratio sampling.

Create src/kassab.jl:

1. assign_strahler_orders!(topology, segments, params)
   AK kernel: for each segment, classify Strahler order based on diameter using params.diameter_bounds.
   Store in topology.strahler_order.

2. sample_asymmetry(params, rng) -> Float64
   Sample from Beta(2.5, 0.8), clamp to [min_asymmetry, 1.0].
   Returns ratio Ds/Dl (smaller/larger daughter diameter).

3. compute_daughter_radii(r_parent, asymmetry, gamma) -> (r_large, r_small)
   From Murray's law + asymmetry ratio:
   r_large = r_parent / (1 + asymmetry^gamma)^(1/gamma)
   r_small = asymmetry * r_large

4. apply_kassab_radii!(tree, params)
   For each bifurcation, sample asymmetry and compute daughter radii.
   Then propagate changes through the tree.

5. Update grow_tree! to use Kassab asymmetry when assigning daughter radii.

Create test/test_kassab.jl:
- Strahler ordering matches hand-computed examples
- Asymmetry samples follow Beta(2.5, 0.8) distribution (KS test)
- Daughter radii satisfy Murray's law with sampled asymmetry
- Order assignments match Kassab diameter bounds exactly
acceptanceCriteria: Strahler ordering computed via AK kernel on diameter array
Asymmetry follows Beta(2.5, 0.8) — verified by KS test
compute_daughter_radii satisfies Murray's law (gamma=7/3)
Growth loop uses Kassab asymmetry for daughter radii
At least 15 tests passing

stories.title: Kassab: Connectivity matrix validation
stories.description:
Implement connectivity matrix construction and validation against Kassab 1993.

Add to src/kassab.jl:

1. build_empirical_connectivity(tree, params) -> Matrix{Float64}
   For each bifurcation in the tree, record (parent_order, daughter1_order, daughter2_order).
   Build the empirical connectivity matrix: C[m,n] = probability that order-m parent has order-n daughter.

2. validate_connectivity(empirical_matrix, kassab_matrix) -> (chi2_stat, p_value)
   Chi-squared test comparing empirical vs Kassab connectivity.

3. constrain_connectivity!(tree, params)
   Post-hoc adjustment: if a bifurcation violates connectivity constraints
   (e.g., order 10 parent with order 2 daughter, which Kassab says probability ~0),
   adjust the daughter radius to fall within a valid order.

Add to test/test_kassab.jl:
- Build connectivity matrix from a generated tree
- Verify matrix rows sum to ~1.0
- After constrain_connectivity!, chi-squared p-value improves
acceptanceCriteria: Empirical connectivity matrix computed correctly
Chi-squared test compares against Kassab matrix
constrain_connectivity! improves p-value
Matrix rows sum to approximately 1.0
At least 10 tests passing

stories.title: Kassab: Integrated constraint enforcement in growth loop
stories.description:
Integrate Kassab constraints into the CCO growth loop so they are enforced during tree construction, not just post-hoc.

Update src/growth.jl:

1. When adding a bifurcation:
   a. Sample asymmetry from Kassab distribution
   b. Compute daughter radii via Murray's law + asymmetry
   c. Assign Strahler orders to new segments
   d. Check connectivity matrix: is this parent-daughter order combination valid?
   e. If invalid, resample asymmetry or adjust radii until valid

2. After adding a bifurcation:
   a. Update Strahler orders for affected segments
   b. Propagate radius changes

3. Length enforcement:
   a. Check that segment length/diameter ratio is near Kassab's 16.8
   b. If too far off, adjust bifurcation point slightly

Update tests to verify Kassab constraints hold during growth.
acceptanceCriteria: Kassab constraints enforced during growth, not post-hoc
Asymmetry distribution maintained throughout growth
Connectivity matrix violations < 5% of bifurcations
L/D ratio within 2 sigma of Kassab's 16.8
Existing tests still pass
At least 10 new tests

stories.title: Barabasi: Chi/rho parameters + junction geometry
stories.description:
Implement Barabasi's junction geometry rules.

Create src/barabasi.jl:

1. compute_chi(parent_radius, node_distance) -> Float64
   chi = 2*pi*parent_radius / node_distance
   Determines whether trifurcation is stable (chi > 0.83).

2. compute_rho(r_small, r_large) -> Float64
   rho = r_small / r_large
   Determines sprouting vs branching.

3. compute_junction_angles(rho, params) -> (angle_large, angle_small)
   If rho < params.sprouting_threshold: SPROUTING
     angle_large = 0 (continues straight), angle_small = pi/2 (perpendicular)
   If rho >= params.sprouting_threshold: BRANCHING
     Both deflect, Omega = params.branching_slope * (rho - rho_th)
     Distribute: angle_small gets more deflection

4. apply_junction_geometry!(tree, bifurc_idx, params)
   Given a bifurcation, compute rho, determine sprouting/branching,
   and rotate daughter segment endpoints to match the correct angles.

5. classify_junction_type(rho, params) -> Symbol
   Returns :sprouting or :branching

Create test/test_barabasi.jl:
- rho < 0.83 -> sprouting angles (0, pi/2)
- rho = 1.0 -> symmetric branching (~60 deg each)
- rho = 0.83 (threshold) -> minimal branching
- chi < 0.83 -> bifurcation, chi > 0.83 -> trifurcation eligible
- Angle distribution on generated tree is bimodal (sprouting peak + branching peak)
acceptanceCriteria: Chi and rho parameters computed correctly
Sprouting regime produces ~90 deg small branch
Branching regime produces symmetric-ish angles for rho~1
Junction type classification is correct
apply_junction_geometry! modifies daughter endpoints correctly
At least 15 tests passing

stories.title: Barabasi: Trifurcation detection + handling
stories.description:
Implement trifurcation (k=4 nodes) as predicted by Barabasi when chi > 0.83.

Update src/barabasi.jl:

1. check_trifurcation_merge(tree, new_bifurc_point, params) -> Union{Nothing, Int32}
   Check if the new bifurcation should merge with a nearby existing bifurcation.
   Search radius = f(parent_radius).
   If a nearby bifurcation exists AND chi > 0.83: return its index for merging.

2. merge_to_trifurcation!(tree, existing_bifurc_idx, new_terminal, params)
   Convert existing bifurcation to trifurcation:
   - The existing bifurcation node now has 3 daughters (child3_id gets populated)
   - Set junction_type to :trifurcation
   - Compute 3-way angle distribution
   - Update topology

3. Update grow_tree! to check for trifurcation before creating bifurcation.

4. compute_trifurcation_angles(r1, r2, r3, params) -> NTuple{3, Float64}
   Three-way angle distribution minimizing surface cost.

Update types.jl if needed to support trifurcations (child3_id already in topology).

Create additional tests:
- Force a scenario where chi > 0.83 -> verify trifurcation created
- Verify topology is correct after trifurcation (3 children)
- Verify Murray's law at trifurcation: r_parent^gamma = r1^gamma + r2^gamma + r3^gamma
acceptanceCriteria: Trifurcation detection works based on chi threshold
merge_to_trifurcation! correctly updates topology to 3 daughters
Murray's law holds at trifurcations (3-way sum)
Growth loop creates trifurcations when appropriate
5-20% of junctions are trifurcations in large trees
At least 10 tests passing

stories.title: Barabasi: Surface cost function + integration into growth
stories.description:
Replace volume cost with surface cost in the CCO growth loop, following Barabasi.

Update src/kamiya.jl:

1. Modify surface_cost_at_t to include proper junction surface estimate:
   Junction surface = f(r_parent, r_daughter1, r_daughter2, angles)
   For tubes: approximate junction surface using the pants decomposition estimate.

2. For sprouting junctions (rho < 0.83):
   Junction surface is smaller (small branch barely disturbs parent surface).

3. For branching junctions (rho >= 0.83):
   Junction surface is larger (both daughters create significant surface).

Update src/growth.jl:
- Apply junction geometry (Barabasi angles) when adding each bifurcation
- The daughter endpoints should reflect the computed angles, not just straight splaying

Integration test:
- Grow a 1000-terminal tree with Barabasi geometry active
- Verify angle distribution is bimodal
- Verify trifurcation percentage is 5-20%
- Verify tree is still valid (no intersections, Murray's law holds)
acceptanceCriteria: Surface cost includes junction surface estimate
Sprouting junctions have lower surface cost than branching
Bifurcation angles reflect Barabasi geometry in the actual tree
1000-terminal tree with Barabasi + Kassab constraints generates without error
Angle distribution is bimodal
At least 10 tests passing

stories.title: Validation framework + diameter/asymmetry tests
stories.description:
Implement statistical validation of generated trees against Kassab data.

Create src/validation.jl:

1. ValidationReport struct — holds all test results

2. validate_tree(tree, params) -> ValidationReport
   Run all validation metrics:

   a. Diameter distribution by Strahler order:
      For each order, extract all segment diameters.
      KS test against Kassab distribution (Normal with Kassab mean, SD).
      Report p-value per order.

   b. Asymmetry ratio distribution:
      For each bifurcation, compute Ds/Dl.
      KS test against Beta(2.5, 0.8).
      Report median (target: 0.72-0.80).

   c. Length/Diameter ratio:
      Compute L/D for all segments.
      Report mean (target: 16.8 +/- 2.3).

   d. Branching angle distribution:
      Compute angles at all bifurcations.
      Report mean, std, and check for bimodality.

3. print_report(report) — formatted summary

4. Use AK kernels for all bulk computations (diameter extraction, angle computation, etc.)

Create test/test_validation.jl:
- Generate a 1000-terminal tree, run validation
- Diameter KS p-values > 0.05 for at least 8/12 orders
- Asymmetry median in [0.72, 0.80]
- L/D ratio mean in [14.5, 19.1]
acceptanceCriteria: ValidationReport struct captures all metrics
KS tests for diameter distribution by order
KS test for asymmetry ratio distribution
L/D ratio statistics computed
Branching angle statistics computed
All bulk computations use AK kernels
At least 15 tests passing

stories.title: Full validation suite: connectivity + trifurcation + P(lambda)
stories.description:
Complete the validation suite with connectivity matrix, trifurcation percentage, and P(lambda) tests.

Add to src/validation.jl:

1. Connectivity matrix validation:
   Build empirical matrix from tree, compare to Kassab via chi-squared.

2. Trifurcation percentage:
   Count trifurcations / (bifurcations + trifurcations).
   Target: 5-20% (from Barabasi blood vessel data).

3. P(lambda) distribution:
   lambda = l/w for each junction (distance between intermediate nodes / link circumference).
   P(lambda -> 0) should be > 0 (indicates stable trifurcations exist).
   Compare shape to Barabasi Fig 3 blood vessel curve.

4. Murray's law compliance:
   For every bifurcation, compute |r_parent^gamma - (r1^gamma + r2^gamma)| / r_parent^gamma.
   Report max deviation and mean deviation.

5. Add all to validate_tree() and print_report().

Create comprehensive tests.
acceptanceCriteria: Connectivity matrix chi-squared test implemented
Trifurcation percentage reported and in [5%, 20%]
P(lambda) distribution computed
Murray's law deviation reported (should be < 1e-6)
Full validate_tree covers all 6+ metrics
At least 10 tests passing

stories.title: Territory partitioning + collision avoidance
stories.description:
Implement territory partitioning for multi-tree forest generation.

Create src/forest.jl:

1. TerritoryMap — 3D voxel grid assigning each region to a tree
   - initialize_territories(domain, tree_configs) -> TerritoryMap
   - Initially partition by proximity to root positions
   - update_territories!(map, trees) — re-partition based on tree growth

2. TreeConfig struct:
   - name::String (e.g., "LAD", "LCX", "RCA")
   - root_position, root_radius, root_direction
   - target_terminals::Int
   - territory_fraction::Float64 (e.g., 0.40 for LAD)

3. sample_in_territory(domain, territory_map, tree_name, rng) -> point
   Sample candidate point within a specific tree's territory.

4. check_inter_tree_collision(point, other_trees, min_distance) -> Bool
   Verify new segment doesn't collide with segments from other trees.
   Uses AK distance kernels against combined segment arrays.

Create test/test_forest.jl:
- Territory initialization correctly partitions space
- Sampling respects territory boundaries
- Inter-tree collision detection works
acceptanceCriteria: TerritoryMap correctly partitions domain among trees
Sampling is confined to correct territory
Inter-tree collision detection uses AK kernels
Territory fractions approximately match target
At least 10 tests passing

stories.title: Multi-tree forest growth (LAD + LCX + RCA)
stories.description:
Implement simultaneous growth of multiple trees to create a complete coronary forest.

Add to src/forest.jl:

1. CoronaryForest struct:
   - trees::Dict{String, VascularTree}
   - territory_map::TerritoryMap
   - params::MorphometricParams

2. generate_coronary_forest(domain, params; tree_configs, rng) -> CoronaryForest
   Round-robin growth: add one terminal to each tree in turn.
   After each round, update territory map.
   Continue until all trees reach target_terminals.

3. coronary_tree_configs() -> Vector{TreeConfig}
   Default LAD (40%), LCX (25%), RCA (35%) configuration.
   Root positions at appropriate coronary ostia locations.

4. validate_forest(forest, params) -> ForestValidationReport
   Validate each tree individually + check inter-tree properties:
   - No inter-tree intersections
   - Territory coverage > 95%
   - Each tree passes individual validation

Create test/test_forest.jl:
- Generate small forest (3 trees, 200 terminals each)
- No inter-tree intersections
- Each tree passes individual validation
- Territory coverage > 90%
acceptanceCriteria: CoronaryForest holds multiple trees with territory map
Round-robin growth produces balanced trees
No inter-tree intersections
coronary_tree_configs provides reasonable defaults
Forest validation covers individual + inter-tree checks
At least 10 tests passing

stories.title: JLD2 save/load + VTP export

stories.title: STL mesh export + graph export
stories.description:
Implement mesh and graph export formats.

Add to src/export.jl:

1. export_stl(tree, filename; circumferential_resolution=16)
   Generate cylinder mesh for each segment using AK kernel:
   - AK.foreachindex: for each segment, generate circumferential_resolution vertices at each end
   - Connect with triangles
   - Junction smoothing (optional)
   Write as binary STL.

2. export_graph_json(tree, filename)
   Export tree topology as JSON graph:
   - Nodes: bifurcations + terminals with position, radius
   - Edges: segments with length, radius, flow, order
   Compatible with network analysis tools.

3. export_csv(tree, filename)
   Flat CSV of all segments for spreadsheet analysis.

Mesh generation MUST use AK.foreachindex for cylinder vertex computation.
